meta.name: GDS-15 Geriatric Depression Scale
meta.description: The GDS-15 is a 15-item self-report scale specifically designed for screening depression in older adults. It uses a simple yes/no format that is easier for elderly respondents to complete, and focuses on non-somatic symptoms of depression to reduce confounding with physical illness.
meta.author: Yesavage et al. (1982)
meta.reference: Yesavage JA, Brink TL, Rose TL, et al. Development and validation of a geriatric depression screening scale: a preliminary report. J Psychiatr Res. 1982-83;17(1):37-49.
meta.instruction: Choose the best answer for how you have felt over the past week.

questions.text: Are you basically satisfied with your life?
Have you dropped many of your activities and interests?
Do you feel that your life is empty?
Are you in good spirits most of the time?
Are you afraid that something bad is going to happen to you?
Do you feel happy most of the time?
Do you prefer to stay at home, rather than going out and doing new things?
Do you feel you have more problems with memory than most?
Do you think it is wonderful to be alive now?
Do you feel pretty worthless the way you are now?
Do you feel full of energy?
Do you feel that your situation is hopeless?
Do you think that most people are better off than you are?

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your score is within the normal range, suggesting no significant depressive symptoms. Your emotional well-being appears stable, which is positive for overall health in later life.
suggestions: Continue engaging in social and physical activities that you enjoy
Maintain regular routines, hobbies, and meaningful social connections
Stay connected with family and friends to maintain emotional well-being
Regular physical activity, even short walks, supports both mental and physical health

ranges.label: Mild Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild depressive symptoms that may be affecting your quality of life. In older adults, depression can sometimes present as fatigue, social withdrawal, or physical complaints rather than overt sadness.
suggestions: Talk to your doctor about how you have been feeling; depression is common and treatable at any age
Stay physically active; even short daily walks can significantly improve mood
Maintain social connections and engage in enjoyable activities
Consider joining a senior community group, class, or volunteer activity

ranges.label: Moderate Depression
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate depressive symptoms that warrant professional attention. These symptoms may be interfering with daily activities, social engagement, and overall quality of life.
suggestions: Consult your healthcare provider for a comprehensive evaluation
Psychotherapy and/or medication can be effective at any age; do not hesitate to explore options
Try to maintain daily routines and regular sleep patterns
Accept support from family and friends; letting others help is a strength, not a weakness
Be aware that some medications or medical conditions can contribute to depressive symptoms

ranges.label: Severe Depression
ranges.description: Your score suggests severe depressive symptoms that are likely significantly affecting your daily life, independence, and well-being. Depression in older adults is very treatable, and seeking help is crucial.
suggestions: Seek medical attention from your healthcare provider as soon as possible
Depression in older adults is very treatable; do not assume it is just part of aging
If you have thoughts of self-harm, please call a crisis hotline immediately (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Inform a family member or trusted person about your feelings
A combination of therapy and medication is often the most effective approach
